issues.description: The MC timing is wrong and internally contradictory. It says Section One is 'one hundred five minutes' but then '60 minutes' (Part A) + '45 minutes' (Part B) = 105... that part is consistent, BUT the real AP Calculus AB exam is Part A = 30 questions in 60 min (no calc), Part B = 15 questions in 45 min (calc) — that matches. The genuine error: it states the overall exam is 'three hours fifteen minutes total.' 105 min (MC) + 90 min (FRQ) = 195 min = 3 hr 15 min — that is correct. So structure numbers actually reconcile. The real critical flaw is the FRQ count framing combined with section 14 (see below); the headline structure here is accurate. Downgrade-on-its-own, but the '45 multiple choice' total is right (30+15). No false belief from this section alone.
issues.fix: No change needed to the numbers here; they reconcile. Keep as-is.

issues.description: The time math misleads. It says 'Ninety minutes, six free response problems — fifteen minutes per problem.' But the exam splits the 90 minutes into Part A (2 problems / 30 min = 15 min each) and Part B (4 problems / 60 min = 15 min each), and CRITICALLY the calculator is COLLECTED after Part A — you cannot return to Part A with the calculator. The advice 'Do parts a and b of every problem first... Then come back for parts c and d' tells the student to defer Part A work, but once the 30-minute Part A window closes the calculator is taken away and Part A cannot be revisited with it. Following this 'come back later' strategy on the calculator-required Part A problems will cost real points. This induces a genuinely bad exam strategy.
issues.fix: Clarify the two FRQ sub-sections are timed and separated: 'Finish BOTH calculator problems (Part A) completely within the first 30 minutes — the calculator is collected and you cannot return to them. THEN apply the a/b-first sweep within Part B's four no-calculator problems.'

issues.description: 'final numerical answers on the calculator section must be at least three decimal places. Two decimals is not enough.' This is stated as a universal rule but applies to the calculator (Part A) FRQ section. The bigger misconception risk: a student may infer they must give 3 decimals EVERYWHERE, including the no-calculator Part B where exact/closed-form answers are expected and decimal approximation can LOSE points or is impossible. Pairing 'the calculator section' loosely with 'must be at least three decimals' without scoping it to numeric-approximation answers can mislead.
issues.fix: Scope it: 'On the calculator part, when an answer is a decimal approximation, give at least three decimal places. On the no-calculator part, leave answers exact (fractions, radicals, expressions) — do not decimalize.'

issues.description: 'If you write the same number twice, you lose a point' overstates a rubric mechanic. On v = t^2 - 4 over [0,3], net displacement and total distance are genuinely different values (-3 vs 23/3), so writing the same number twice would simply be a wrong answer, not a special 'penalty for repeating.' The phrasing implies a graders-penalize-duplication rule that does not exist; the real point is that displacement uses integral of v and distance uses integral of |v|.
issues.fix: Reframe: 'These are different questions with different answers — distance requires the absolute value. If you report displacement for both, the distance part is just wrong.'

issues.description: 'A setup with no answer often scores one of two points' presents a made-up fixed ratio. FRQ parts are not uniformly 2-point; some are 1, 2, 3, or more, and setup credit varies by part. Stating '1 of 2' as a general rule can give a false expectation of partial-credit value.
issues.fix: Generalize: 'A correct setup integral with no final answer often still earns partial credit — write it even when you cannot finish.'

issues.description: The model answer says justification needs 'the citation of the Fundamental Theorem' as a required rubric piece. Real AP rubrics for 'where is g maximized' typically require identifying the critical point and the sign change of g' (= f) — explicitly naming the FTC by name is good practice but is generally NOT a separately scored requirement. Telling students 'skip any one of them and the point is gone' overstates the FTC-citation requirement and could cause anxiety/false belief that omitting the words 'Fundamental Theorem' loses a point.
issues.fix: Soften: 'Name the location, justify with the sign change of g prime = f. Citing the Fundamental Theorem strengthens it but the load-bearing pieces are the where and the sign-change reason.'

issues.description: Lists 'six free response patterns that show up every single year' as if all six appear annually in fixed slots. Patterns rotate and not all six appear every year (there are 6 FRQ slots but the type mix varies). 'Show up every single year' / 'show up every year' could create a false expectation that, e.g., a slope-field DiffEq is guaranteed on this year's exam.
issues.fix: Hedge: 'These six pattern TYPES recur across years; expect most of them, but the exact mix rotates — recognize the type rather than memorizing a fixed slot.'

notes: Mostly solid, but the Part-A calculator-collection timing gap in 14_time_budget is a genuinely point-costing strategy error that warrants a critical verdict.
